pattern: Every path is a fixed sequence of right and down moves, so the count is the binomial choice of where the down moves fall among the total steps.
hints: Reaching the corner of an m by n grid always takes the same number of moves: (m-1) downs and (n-1) rights, in some order.
The number of distinct orderings of those identical moves is a single combination, but you can also build it up cell by cell.
Either compute C(m+n-2, m-1) directly, or fill a table where each cell's path count is the sum of the cell above and the cell to its left, starting the first row and column at 1.

edge_inputs.description: single cell grid has exactly one path
single row only allows moving right
single column only allows moving down
answer is symmetric in m and n
a tall-and-narrow grid whose count stays under the guaranteed cap
a wide-and-short grid whose count stays under the guaranteed cap
near-square grid kept small enough that the count fits the guarantee

stress.description: random grid dimensions chosen so the path count stays within the problem's guaranteed 2*10^9 cap (exactly representable, so python and javascript agree)
stress.note: the problem guarantees the answer is at most 2*10^9; we mirror that so the float-based JS reference is exact
